sections.heading: Most regions cluster around wanting no more children
sections.body: The national share of women who wanted no more children was 49.4 percent in 2025, and most regions sat near or above that level. Bicol Region, MIMAROPA, and Negros Island Region formed the upper cluster at 57.0, 56.9, and 56.7 percent, while Central Visayas followed at 54.7 percent. That pattern suggests the strongest regional differences are not only about one standout region, but about a broad group where stopping childbearing is already the dominant preference.

sections.heading: BARMM stands apart from the national pattern
sections.body: BARMM had the lowest share wanting no more children in 2025, at 27.3 percent, far below the national level of 49.4 percent. It also had the highest undecided share at 27.8 percent, making undecided preferences about as large as wanting no more children. This is a different regional profile from places where wanting no more is clearly the main response, and it points to a less settled pattern of fertility preferences.

sections.heading: Zamboanga Peninsula combines low stopping preference with delayed childbearing
sections.body: Zamboanga Peninsula¹ was near the low end for wanting no more children, at 39.9 percent in 2025. Unlike BARMM, its largest offset was not undecided preference but wanting another child later, where it ranked highest nationally at 23.5 percent. That combination makes the region look less like a simple low-stopping outlier and more like a place where delayed childbearing remains a large part of the preference mix.

sections.heading: The largest regional shifts went in different directions
sections.body: Cagayan Valley had the largest increase in wanting no more children, rising from 39.8 percent in 2022 to 49.4 percent in 2025. BARMM also rose sharply, from 18.6 to 27.3 percent, though it remained the lowest region in 2025. In the other direction, Zamboanga Peninsula declined from 54.9 to 39.9 percent, a much larger drop than the declines in Bicol Region, Western Visayas, or Eastern Visayas.

sections.heading: The national mix changed little, but uncertainty grew
sections.body: At the national level, wanting no more children was nearly unchanged, moving from 48.8 percent in 2022 to 49.4 percent in 2025. Wanting another child soon and wanting another child later both edged down, from 13.9 to 13.4 percent and from 17.4 to 16.2 percent. The clearest movement was in the undecided category, which increased from 8.1 to 10.0 percent, even as sterilization and declared infecund shares declined slightly.

sections.heading: Family size marks the sharpest transition
sections.body: The number-of-living-children table shows a steep shift from wanting another child to wanting no more, with current pregnancies included in the count. Among women with no living children, 54.9 percent wanted another child soon and only 6.2 percent wanted no more. By two children, wanting no more had reached 57.5 percent, and it climbed further to 72.8 percent among women with six or more children.

takeaways: Wanting no more children was the dominant national preference in 2025, but regional levels ranged from 27.3 percent in BARMM to 57.0 percent in Bicol Region.
BARMM stood apart because undecided preference was almost as common as wanting no more children.
Preferences shifted sharply by family size, with wanting no more children becoming the majority response once women had two living children.
footnotes: ¹ Includes the province of Sulu in 2025.
